formulas.name: Separation of Powers
formulas.notes: The powers of the legislative, executive, and judicial branches of a government are separated to prevent any one branch from becoming too powerful.

formulas.name: Checks and Balances
formulas.notes: Each branch of government has powers that allow it to limit the actions of the other branches, preventing any one branch from becoming too powerful.

formulas.name: Federalism
formulas.notes: A system of government in which power is divided between a central authority and smaller regional governments.

formulas.name: Judicial Review
formulas.notes: The power of a court to declare a law or government action unconstitutional and therefore invalid.

formulas.name: Independent Judiciary
formulas.notes: A judiciary that is free from the influence of the executive and legislative branches of government.

formulas.name: Separate Electorate
formulas.notes: A system in which members of a particular group, such as ethnic or religious groups, elect their own representatives.

formulas.name: Council of Ministers
formulas.notes: A group of ministers who advise the head of government and are responsible for implementing government policies.

formulas.name: Accountability
formulas.notes: The responsibility of those in power to be answerable to the public and to act in the public interest.
